metadata.description: Comprehensive transmission and distribution technology database aligned with the Open Energy Ontology (OEO). Covers AC/DC electrical networks, gas/H2/CO2 pipelines, and district heating networks. Designed for PyPSA, Calliope, OSeMOSYS, and ADOPTNet0 energy system modeling.

notes.capex_normalization: For line/cable/pipeline technologies, capex_usd_per_kw is normalized over a representative corridor length (stated in instance_name). Primary engineering cost expression is also noted (USD/km or USD/MW-km).
notes.efficiency_percent: For electrical lines: (1 - total losses for stated corridor length) × 100. For pipelines: pipeline transport efficiency including compressor parasitic load over stated distance.
notes.ramping_rate: For electrical lines: limited in practice by protection relay speed (~999%/min effective). For pipelines: limited by compressor acceleration and line-packing (~0.5–5%/min).
notes.co2_factor: All transmission infrastructure has zero direct operational CO2 emissions. Upstream emissions from compressor fuel (gas pipelines) counted in fuel chain.

primary_sources: IEA World Energy Outlook 2023 – Grid Investment
IRENA Future of Solar PV 2019 – Grid Costs
CIGRE Technical Brochure TB 812 – HVDC Economics
European Commission Hydrogen Backbone Study 2021 (GIE/Hydrogen Europe)
Euroheat & Power District Heating Cost Study 2022

technologies.description: High-voltage alternating current (HVAC) overhead transmission lines on lattice steel towers. Operating at 220–400 kV (Europe) or 230–765 kV (North America). Dominant technology for bulk power transmission up to ~600 km. Reactive power compensation required at longer distances.

technologies.description: High-voltage direct current (HVDC) overhead transmission lines using VSC or LCC converter technology. Lower line losses than HVAC at distances > ~600 km. Enables asynchronous grid interconnection and precise power flow control. Converter stations are significant cost items.

technologies.description: High-voltage AC underground XLPE cables installed in direct-buried or ducted trenches. Used in dense urban areas, protected landscapes, and short sub-sea crossings where overhead lines are not feasible. Significantly higher cost than overhead lines; shorter practical lengths due to reactive power issues.

technologies.description: Power transformers serving bulk transmission and sub-transmission networks at 66 kV and above. Includes HV/HV, UHV/HV, step-up and step-down transformers at major grid substations, phase-shifting transformers (PSTs/FACTS) for active power flow control, HVDC converter transformers, offshore platform units, and mobile emergency spares. Nearest OEO class: OEO_00000420 (electrical equipment / power apparatus).

instances.instance_name: Transformer – 100 MVA, 220/33 kV HV/MV (Substation, Current 2024)
instances.reference_source: IEC / IEEE Transformer Standards; ENTSO-E Asset Data 2021

instances.reference_source: CIGRE WG A2.43 – Transformer Design Life 2019

Transformer – 132 kV Offshore Platform 200 MVA (2024)

technologies.description: Power transformers stepping voltage down from medium-voltage (11–33 kV) networks to low-voltage (0.4–11 kV) for final delivery to end customers. Includes conventional oil-type pad-mount and pole-mount units, amorphous-core energy-efficient designs, dry-type (cast-resin) units for indoor and wind-farm collector stations, on-load tap-changer (OLTC) smart variants, solid-state transformers (SST) for future smart-grid integration, and rail traction transformers. Dominant asset class in distribution networks by count (hundreds of thousands per country). Nearest OEO class: OEO_00000420 (electrical equipment / power apparatus).

technologies.description: New-build or repurposed steel pipelines transporting pure hydrogen gas at 30–100 bar. Higher compressor energy requirements than CH4 (different molecular weight and viscosity). European Hydrogen Backbone initiative targets 4000 km of H2 pipelines by 2030 and 28,000 km by 2040.

technologies.description: Carbon dioxide transport pipelines for CCS/CCU value chains, typically operating in supercritical dense-phase (above 73 bar) to minimise pipeline diameter. Connect capture sites to geological storage locations. efficiency_percent = pipeline transport efficiency (fraction of CO2 delivered). capex normalised per kW of associated capture input.

technologies.description: Insulated pipe networks distributing chilled water (5–12 °C supply / 14–18 °C return) from centralised chillers to buildings for air conditioning. Common in dense urban districts, airports, and university campuses. Avoids per-building chillers; improves COP via large-scale efficiency gains. efficiency_percent reflects pipe heat-gain losses and pump parasitic consumption.

technologies.description: Road transport of compressed gaseous hydrogen (200–700 bar) in tube-trailer bundles or cryogenic liquid tankers. Bridging technology before pipeline infrastructure is deployed. Capacity per trip: 200–1000 kg H2 (gaseous tube trailer) or 3000–5000 kg (liquid tanker). efficiency_percent = delivered H2 fraction (accounting for ullage and boil-off). capex_usd_per_kw normalised per kW of refuelling-station equivalent throughput.

technologies.description: Low-to-medium pressure pipelines (1–16 bar) transporting raw or upgraded biogas (biomethane) from production sites (digesters, landfills) to end users or grid injection points. Biogas contains 50–70% CH4 + CO2; biomethane is upgraded to >95% CH4. Compressor stations at 20–60 km intervals. efficiency_percent reflects pipeline transport efficiency including compressor parasitic load.

technologies.description: Road transport of solid biomass (wood chips, pellets, agricultural residues, straw) in tipper trucks or walking-floor trailers. Dominant mode for distances < 200 km. Payload: 20–30 t dry matter per truck. efficiency_percent ≈ delivered energy fraction (accounting for fuel combustion in transport). capex_usd_per_kw normalised per average throughput kW.

Among the lowest-cost transport modes per unit energy. efficiency_percent reflects viscous-flow pump parasitic load over stated corridor.

technologies.description: Pressurised insulated pipelines transporting industrial or district steam (100–250 °C, 3–25 bar gauge) from centralised boilers or CHP plants to industrial consumers, hospitals, or dense urban blocks. Higher temperature than hot-water district heating; used where process heat quality is required. efficiency_percent reflects heat losses in distribution at operating temperature. Condensate return credited.

technologies.description: High-temperature (80–400 °C) insulated pipelines and distribution systems transporting thermal energy within industrial parks, special economic zones, and factory complexes. Media include pressurised hot water, steam, synthetic thermal oil (Therminol/Dowtherm), and molten salt. Distinct from residential district heating in temperature range, pipe materials (carbon-steel, stainless), and operating pressures (6–100 bar). Enable industrial symbiosis by exchanging waste heat between co-located industries. OEO class: OEO_00020005 (thermal energy network / district heat).

technologies.description: Dedicated pipeline infrastructure distributing geothermal brine, hot water, or steam from geothermal wellheads to end users. Encompasses low-enthalpy hydrothermal systems (40–80 °C, direct-use district heating), medium-enthalpy systems (80–150 °C), high-enthalpy volcanic systems (>150 °C), enhanced geothermal systems (EGS with engineered reservoirs), and deep aquifer networks. Includes brine return pipelines, plate heat exchangers at the wellhead, and reinjection infrastructure. Distinct from district heating networks by the primary energy source and the presence of brine-handling and corrosion-resistant materials. OEO class: OEO_00020005 (thermal energy network / district heat).

technologies.description: Interface equipment between primary heat distribution networks and secondary building/consumer circuits. Includes plate heat exchangers (PHEs), compact heat interface units (HIUs), mixing stations, pressure-breaking substations, and smart metering-integrated units. Thermally decouple the primary network (e.g., 80–120 °C) from secondary low-temperature circuits (40–60 °C). Critical for 4th- and 5th-generation district heating where return temperatures must be minimized for system efficiency. Nearest OEO class: OEO_00000420 (electrical/thermal equipment apparatus); precise OEO heat-exchanger subclass pending OEO extension.

Lower voltage than HV transmission cables; CAPEX normalised per km and rated capacity. Critical last-mile infrastructure between the transmission system and end-user distribution transformers. OEO class reused from power line concept (OEO_00000047).

technologies.description: Low voltage (0.4-1 kV) underground and overhead cables for last-mile electricity distribution to residential and commercial consumers. Includes three-phase and single-phase configurations. High cost per kW due to small capacity per run. Substantial portion of total grid infrastructure investment; increasingly critical for EV charging integration and prosumer/microgrid connections. OEO class: OEO_00000047 (power line concept).

technologies.description: High-voltage to medium-voltage substations forming the primary nodes of the electricity grid. Comprise power transformers, HV/MV switchgear, busbars, protection and control systems, metering, and civil works. Categories: Grid Supply Points (GSP) connected to the transmission system, primary substations (132/33 kV, 110/10 kV), zone substations (33/11 kV), and compact GIS substations for urban and offshore deployments. CAPEX normalised to rated throughput capacity. Nearest OEO class: power transformer (OEO_00000420); precise OEO substation class pending ontology update.

technologies.description: Secondary distribution substations transforming medium voltage (6-36 kV) to low voltage (0.4 kV) for final consumer supply. Include the transformer, MV switchgear (ring main unit or circuit breaker), LV distribution board, metering, and civil enclosure. Deployed as pole-mounted, pad-mounted, kiosk (ground-mounted), or underground-vault types depending on load density and urban constraints. CAPEX normalised to rated LV output capacity. Nearest OEO class: power transformer (OEO_00000420).

technologies.description: Voltage-source converter-based FACTS device providing fast dynamic reactive power compensation for voltage stability, flicker mitigation, and grid support. Response within one electrical cycle (<20 ms). Deployed at transmission and distribution nodes, renewable energy connection points, and industrial facilities with rapidly varying loads. Rated in MVAr (reactive power), normalised here to MW apparent power capacity. OEO class: OEO_00000047 (electricity grid element); precise FACTS/reactive-compensation class pending OEO extension.

technologies.description: Thyristor-controlled FACTS device providing variable reactive power compensation through thyristor-controlled reactors (TCR) and thyristor-switched capacitors (TSC). Slower response than STATCOM (1-2 electrical cycles) but cost-effective for large reactive power requirements. Widely deployed for industrial arc furnace applications, HV transmission voltage support, and long HVAC line reactive balance. Rated in MVAr, normalised here to MW apparent power. OEO class: OEO_00000047 (electricity grid element).

technologies.description: AC/DC and DC/AC converter stations forming the terminal equipment of HVDC transmission links. Each HVDC link requires two converter stations (one at each end). Types: Line-Commutated Converters (LCC, thyristor-based, low losses, requires strong AC grid) and Voltage Source Converters (VSC, IGBT-based MMC architecture, enables black-start and weak AC grid connection). Modelled separately from HVDC cable or overhead line (see hvdc_overhead_lines, hvdc_subsea_cables) to allow independent sizing and costing of converter infrastructure.

technologies.description: High- and medium-voltage switchgear for grid protection, isolation, and fault clearance. Includes circuit breakers, disconnectors, earthing switches, and bus sections integrated in Air-Insulated Switchgear (AIS) outdoor yards or Gas-Insulated Switchgear (GIS) indoor enclosures. GIS is 5-10x more compact and preferred for urban and offshore environments; AIS is lower cost for spacious greenfield sites. SF6-free eco GIS is growing due to EU F-Gas regulation. CAPEX normalised to rated throughput per bay. Nearest OEO class: OEO_00000420 (electrical equipment); precise switching-device class pending OEO extension.
